name: Afrasiyab
description: Afrasiyab is a pivotal figure in Persian mythology, primarily featured in the Shahnameh (Book of Kings) by Ferdowsi. He is the legendary king of Turan, a region often associated with Central Asia, and a perennial adversary of the Iranian kings. Afrasiyab is portrayed as a powerful and cunning warrior, a master strategist, and a formidable enemy of the heroes of Iran, particularly those associated with the Kayanian dynasty. His reign is marked by constant warfare and conflict with Iran, driven by a deep-seated rivalry and territorial ambitions. He is often depicted as a symbol of chaos and barbarism, contrasting with the perceived order and civilization of Iran. Afrasiyab's actions frequently involve treachery and deceit, such as the murder of Siyavash, a beloved Iranian prince, which fuels a long and bloody cycle of revenge. Despite his negative portrayal, Afrasiyab is a complex character, possessing both strength and vulnerability. His ultimate defeat and death at the hands of Kay Khosrow mark a significant turning point in the epic, symbolizing the triumph of good over evil and the eventual establishment of peace and justice.
subtitle: King of Turan, Iran's Nemesis

keyMyths.title: The Murder of Siyavash
keyMyths.description: Afrasiyab orchestrates the murder of Siyavash, a noble Iranian prince who had sought refuge in Turan. This act of treachery ignites a long-lasting feud between Iran and Turan.

keyMyths.title: Afrasiyab's Battles with Kay Khosrow
keyMyths.description: Afrasiyab engages in numerous battles with Kay Khosrow, the righteous king of Iran, seeking to expand his territory and defeat his rival. These conflicts are central to the narrative.

keyMyths.title: Afrasiyab's Demise
keyMyths.description: Afrasiyab is eventually captured and killed by Kay Khosrow, fulfilling the prophecy of revenge for Siyavash's death. His death marks the end of his reign of terror.

sources.author: Ferdowsi
sources.description: The primary source for Afrasiyab's mythology and deeds.
sources.text: The primary source for Afrasiyab's mythology and deeds.

sources.author: Dick Davis
